title: Day 01 Quiz — React Foundations
description: Verify you understand modern JavaScript basics, TypeScript purpose, JSX, components, and Vite before moving to Day 02.

questions.prompt: Why do production React teams prefer const over let for most bindings?

options.text: const makes values deeply immutable in JavaScript
const prevents accidental reassignment and signals intent
const is required for JSX to compile
const improves runtime performance measurably

questions.explanation: const prevents rebinding the variable, which reduces accidental mutation bugs. It does not make objects immutable — that matters later when updating React state.

questions.prompt: JSX is identical to HTML and can be pasted directly from an HTML file into a React component without changes.
questions.explanation: JSX resembles HTML but uses different attribute names (className, htmlFor), requires closing tags, and is compiled to JavaScript. Some HTML patterns are invalid in JSX.
Template literals embed expressions. join creates a single string with the separator between array items.

questions.prompt: What problem does React primarily solve for large interactive applications?

options.text: Replacing the need for a backend API
Managing complex UI state with declarative components instead of manual DOM manipulation
Eliminating the need for CSS
Making JavaScript strongly typed by default

questions.explanation: React's core value is declarative UI composition and predictable updates. TypeScript handles typing; CSS still exists; backends are separate concerns.

questions.prompt: Which are valid reasons teams adopt TypeScript in React codebases? Select all that apply.

options.text: Catch prop type mismatches during development
Document component contracts through types
Guarantee zero runtime bugs
Improve refactor safety in large codebases

questions.explanation: TypeScript catches many errors early and improves maintainability, but it cannot guarantee zero runtime bugs — logic errors and external data still need validation.

questions.prompt: In JSX, use {{blank1}} instead of class, and {{blank2}} instead of for on labels.
questions.explanation: JSX compiles to JavaScript where class and for are reserved keywords, so React uses className and htmlFor.

questions.prompt: Complete the named export import for a Navbar component exported as `export function Navbar`.
questions.explanation: Named exports must be imported with the same name in curly braces. Default exports would use import Navbar from '...' without braces.

questions.prompt: Why is Vite commonly chosen over older React tooling for new projects?

options.text: Vite removes the need for npm
Vite provides faster dev server startup and HMR using native ES modules
Vite only works with class components
Vite automatically writes components for you

questions.explanation: Vite's dev experience — near-instant server start and fast HMR — is the primary reason teams adopt it. It still uses npm and does not generate components.

questions.prompt: In one or two sentences, explain what the Virtual DOM helps React do.
questions.sampleAnswer: React keeps a lightweight in-memory representation of the UI. When state changes, it diffs the new tree against the previous one and applies minimal updates to the real DOM.
questions.explanation: Interviewers want the diffing/reconciliation idea, not a deep algorithm explanation on Day 1.

questions.prompt: A component renders nothing. The function uses curly braces and contains JSX but no return. What is the most likely fix?

options.text: Install React Router
Add an explicit return statement or use parentheses for implicit return
Change let to const
Rename the file to .jsx

questions.explanation: Arrow functions with a block body need return. This is one of the most common Day 1 bugs.
